title: 1x3 and 1x4 Wood Box Tray
summary: A shallow decorative tray: three 1x4s glued into a panel, framed with 1x3 sides. A first-project glue-up you can do with a saw and a nailer.
description:
A simple, good-looking decorative tray — the kind that holds a candle and a couple of books on a coffee table, or corrals clutter on a counter. Three 1x4 boards glued edge to edge make the bottom, framed with 1x3 sides standing proud to form a lip. Add handles if you want to carry it.

The only technique is the glue-up: join the three 1x4s edge to edge and let them fully set before you do anything else, because a tray bottom that hasn't cured will spring apart when you nail the sides on. Then measure the actual combined width of your glued panel and cut the end trim to match — nominal math won't be exact once three boards are joined.

Stain suits it better than paint, and a couple of coats of a wipe-on finish protect it from candle wax and coffee rings. Under an hour of work plus drying time, and it makes a solid gift.
timeLabel: under 1 hr plus glue and stain drying

tools.note: Crosscuts; a miter saw or hand saw works.
Clamp the edge glue-up while it sets.

cutList.note: Cut to match your glued panel width (~10-1/2").

steps.title: Glue up the bottom and add end trim
steps.body: Glue the three 1x4 boards edge to edge and let them fully set — don't rush this, a bottom that hasn't cured springs apart when you nail into it. Once set, measure the combined width of the glued panel, cut the 1x3 end boards to match, and attach with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue.

steps.title: Add the sides and finish
steps.body: Measure and cut the side pieces to fit and nail and glue them to the sides, forming the tray lip. Fill nail holes, sand, and stain — a couple of coats of a wipe-on finish protect it from wax and rings. Add handles if you want to carry it.

images.alt: Shallow decorative wood box tray with framed sides
